meta.title: EMSE — Epidemiology-based Mortality Score in Status Epilepticus (EACE)
meta.authors: Leitinger M, Höller Y, Kalss G, Rohracher A, Novak HF, Höfler J, Dobesberger J, Kuchukhidze G, Trinka E
meta.outcome: Risk of a poor outcome (in-hospital death) after status epilepticus, from the EMSE-EACE combination (aetiology, age, comorbidity, EEG). Point values are literature-derived mortality rates; the total is compared with the cut-off of 64.
meta.source_note: EMSE: Leitinger et al., Neurocrit Care 2015 (doi 10.1007/s12028-014-0080-y, PMID 25412806). Point tables from Figure 2 (EMSE-EACE evaluation sheet). Cut-off 64 = lowest non-survivor EMSE-EACE score in the derivation cohort. In head-to-head studies EMSE-EACE matches or exceeds STESS for mortality prediction (Yuan et al., Epilepsia 2022, doi 10.1111/epi.17442).
meta.cff_info:
What this is. The Epidemiology-based Mortality Score in Status Epilepticus (EMSE) predicts a poor outcome (in-hospital death) after status epilepticus. Unlike STESS, each item's points are the published mortality rate (%) for that feature, so the score is more individualised. This calculator implements the validated four-item version, EMSE-EACE (aEtiology, Age, Comorbidity, EEG).

How it is scored. Add the points for the single worst aetiology, the age band, the Charlson Comorbidity Index (×10), and the worst EEG pattern. The total is compared with the cut-off of 64: a score ≥64 indicates a high risk of a poor outcome, below 64 is favourable.

How well it works. EMSE-EACE has good discrimination (pooled AUC 0.77; Yuan 2022) and a high negative predictive value — a score below 64 reliably identifies survivors. Its positive predictive value varies across cohorts (~40–69%), so a high score raises concern but is not a certainty of death.

Caveats. The cut-off of 64 was defined in a small, explorative derivation cohort (92 non-hypoxic patients) as the lowest score among those who died, so it is optimistic in that sample; use the total and cut-off as a risk indicator, not an exact probability, and always apply clinical judgement. Comorbidity uses the Charlson index (CCI × 10).

source_link.label: PubMed
source_link.title: Leitinger et al., Neurocrit Care 2015 — EMSE

predictors.name: Aetiology (worst single cause)
predictors.assess: The single underlying cause of the status epilepticus (score one stratum). Points are the published mortality rate for that aetiology. 'Acute' cerebrovascular disease or brain trauma = within the past 2 weeks; otherwise use the 'remote' category.

predictors.name: Age
predictors.assess: Patient age band (score one stratum). Points are the published age-specific mortality rate. EMSE age strata were derived for adults; younger patients contribute 0.

predictors.name: Charlson Comorbidity Index (total)
predictors.assess: The patient's total Charlson Comorbidity Index (CCI). EMSE enters comorbidity as CCI × 10 (each CCI point ≈ 10% mortality). Compute the CCI separately (e.g. myocardial infarction, heart failure, diabetes = 1 each; hemiplegia, any tumour, moderate/severe renal disease = 2 each; moderate/severe liver disease = 3; metastatic tumour or AIDS = 6). Do not double-count the aetiology already chosen above.

predictors.name: Worst EEG pattern
predictors.assess: The single worst EEG pattern (score only worst stratum). LPDs = lateralized periodic discharges (≥7 per 10 s); GPDs = generalized periodic discharges (≥9 per 10 s); ASIDs = after-status ictal discharges; spontaneous (non-medically induced) burst suppression scores highest.

model.note: EMSE-EACE is a THRESHOLD score: add aetiology + age + (Charlson×10) + worst EEG, then compare with the cut-off 64. ≥64 = high risk of poor outcome (death); <64 = favourable (high NPV). The two mortality figures shown (~8% below cut-off, ~50% at/above) are approximate, validation-informed bands — the derivation cut-off (lowest non-survivor score, n=92) is optimistic and PPV varies 40–69% across cohorts. Read the EMSE total and the cut-off, not an exact probability.
model.viz_info: Each item — aetiology, age, Charlson comorbidity (×10) and worst EEG pattern — adds points (waterfall) whose values are literature mortality rates. The EMSE-EACE total is shown above; the donut shows the approximate mortality band for being below vs at/above the cut-off of 64.

provenance.source: EMSE-EACE point values taken verbatim from Leitinger et al., Neurocrit Care 2015, Figure 2 (evaluation sheet) — aetiology (CNS-anomalies 2 … anoxia 65), age (21–30:1 … >80:10), comorbidity = Charlson CCI × 10, EEG (none 0; LPDs/GPDs/ASIDs 40; spontaneous burst suppression 60). Cut-off 64 (EMSE-EACE) = lowest non-survivor score in the derivation cohort (Results, p.277). PDF in 0_inbox/papers/status_epilepticus/. Head-to-head performance: Yuan et al., Epilepsia 2022 (AUC 0.77).
provenance.notes: Formula kind, type=lookup: lp = Σ(points) with comorbidity coef=10 (CCI×10), all others coef=1; band split at 64. show_lp displays the EMSE-EACE total. '< 21 years'=0 added for usability (EMSE age table starts at 21–30; younger age contributes ~0). Band mortality (~8% / ~50%) is a validation-informed approximation, flagged approx and caveated (derivation cut-off optimistic, PPV 40–69% across cohorts); EMSE is a threshold classifier, not a per-total probability.
